title: The Need for Sangha
subtitle: Spiritual Friendship and the Economy of Generosity
epigraph: The whole of the spiritual life is good companionship, admirable friendship, admirable camaraderie.
paragraphs: I want to say a few words about Sangha.

The word Sangha traditionally — in the East, anyway — refers only to the monastic orders, the monks and the nuns. But in the West it has become a self-referential word, where Buddhists refer to themselves as the Sangha. It has been widened out to include everybody. And that shows the effect of the secularisation of Buddhism and the growing role of lay people.

When the Buddha decided that he would teach, there was some doubt in his mind — maybe over the subtlety of the teaching, who knows — but anyway, he went back to his companions. And in the first two talks where he introduces his teaching, it seems all of them became fully liberated.

We have to presume they'd done a lot of work beforehand, as he had. He'd done all the practices of concentration, practices of self-mortification — which he didn't gain much from, apart from suffering. And then, of course, he went off by himself, and his search turned around upon itself. Instead of seeking happiness, he began to investigate how he was causing suffering. It was that which led him to his awakening. That's why we say he self-awakened.

There was lots of practice at that time. Lots of men — mainly men, but also some women — were leaving society and living as samanas, the homeless ones. The Buddha didn't start a tradition. It had long been there.

Within that society it was all part of a great movement. Politically, there was a movement away from tribal governance, where you had elders and a chief elder — that was like the Buddha's father of the Sakya tribe — and it was moving towards monarchy. Actually, the Sakyas, this little group of people just on the edge of Nepal under the Himalayas, were under the local king of Kosala. That would have been Pasenadi at the time. They were vassals. In other words, the system was becoming what we would recognise as medieval.
When the Buddha began to teach, certain people — men at first, but then women — wanted to join him. We call it the first ordination. He just said, ehi passiko, which means 'come and try.'

There's a story about him rejecting women for the order, which I'm sure you've come across. These court ladies, including his stepmother, came to join the order and to prove they were up to the raw life of the monastic order — where you just live out in the forest, really, without any of the commodities of life. When he came to them, he didn't think they'd make it, so he denies entry. It's only when Ananda says to him, 'Do you think women can become liberated?' and he says yes, that he accepts them.

But there are definitely little tales in the scriptures that he was accepting women long before that. This is really, I suppose, the growth of — well, the effect of patrimony on society, even on the order.

So he was accepting people, and then once he had a group of men and women who were fully liberated, the ordination procedure was to take refuge in him as the teacher, as the living embodiment of the Dhamma — the teaching he was giving — and then the Sangha, which were these people who had intuited Nibbāna at some level, some of them fully liberated. That's become the lay ordination now. When somebody wants to join the Buddhist religion, the Buddhist faith or whatever, you go through a little ceremony.

But then of course, after about twenty years, there were men and women who joined who weren't quite at the usual standard and were doing terrible and naughty things. So he had to establish rules. It shows that he was very much a pragmatist. He didn't have some ideal as to where he wanted the order to be. The rules came as the monks and nuns did something which was considered not right, not correct.

Some of the offences were like thieving, or any sexual intercourse — monks made those mistakes. For the first monk who committed such an offence, he didn't have the penalty of being kicked out of the order. And then it goes all the way down to having things you shouldn't have. There are ceremonies where you give it up, where you have to hand it back, things like that.

You've got 227 of these rules, and many more that were created as the order went along. When you look at them, they're sectioned out in order of seriousness. But they're all higgledy-piggledy, and some of them don't pertain anymore — like not sitting on woollen rugs. Presumably wool in those days was something quite precious.
The ordination process then became quite complicated. People were joining because they were in debt to the government, so a rule was made: you couldn't join if you were in debt.

There was a physician called Jīvaka, very famous at the time, who offered his medical services free to order members. So sick men and women joined the order to get this free treatment. That sounds pretty straightforward, doesn't it? So you can't join the order if you're sick. Now, that's not an absolute rule. If you join a particular group of monks or nuns, they can decide to accept you. But if they think your illness is going to undermine them financially or physically — because you have to look after people — they can refuse.

Once you're in, though, you really do have to be looked after. There's no way out. And of course, they didn't always do that at the time. When the Buddha came across somebody who was really sick — diarrhoea and vomiting and all that, and nobody wanted to treat him — he did it himself. He did it with anger. Then he turned to the monks and said, 'You haven't got a mother and father who's going to look after you if you don't look after people.' He berated them that way.

So you have the order. Now, in the East the order still fills a real role. Virtually all meditation teachers are monks and nuns. You do get lay people, but it's almost considered an encroachment on the monk's role. The people who know about the Dhamma, who study the Dhamma, and who teach the Dhamma are, for the most part, monks and nuns. And the ones who live by way of that relationship of alms-taking are the monks and nuns.

The Buddha wanted that relationship — so I'm coming back to this whole idea about relationship, about attitude, about love. He didn't want the monks and nuns to be self-supporting. He wanted them to continue to be alms-takers, dependent on lay people for their well-being, for everything they have. And the other side of the gift was that lay people would go to the monks for spiritual advice, for spiritual teachings.
So there was this economy of generosity. The one gave to the other as and when was needed.

And of course, there's this whole idea of karma — that by giving, by generosity, you accumulate goodness power. Now, I know many people in the East do conceptualise it into some sort of bank account, so that when you die you go to a beautiful heaven because you gave all this food to monks. But the basic psychology is correct, because remember: whenever you're generous, you're giving something up that you could have used for yourself. Giving up some of your wealth, giving up some of your time. And to do that is renunciation.

The process of liberation is the process of renunciation. Don't confuse that with self-mortification. Renunciation means letting go of attachments. So every time you give something, you're making a step towards your own liberation.

The commentaries make it clear that generosity leads to liberation by way of renouncing what you would have used for your own personal satisfactions and joy, and giving it to somebody else. As you do that, your goodness power — that's the proper translation of puñña — grows. And that goodness power manifests in your growing desire to liberate yourself from suffering.

Now, we have a situation in the West where, actually, some of the best teachers are lay people. And most of the monks and nuns either can't teach or don't want to — which is true also in the East. It's not as though just because you become a monastic you're going to be a great teacher. Far from it. And you might not want to be. There are other roles you can play within the order. You don't have to play a role at all.

When it comes to academics, the understanding of the scriptures and what not, there's massive, brilliant work being done. Some of the best books on Buddhism now are written by people who are PhDs and so forth. They're not monks at all. They don't belong to a monastic order.
So two of the major roles traditionally played by monastics in the East — teaching the actual practice and teaching the actual Dhamma — have been, as it were, taken over by lay people.

Now there comes a real problem. Why would you want to support a monk if the economy of generosity has disappeared? I'm still dependent on you, but you're no longer dependent on me. Something is undermining that interdependency. There was a fair swap: you fed me and I told you how to practise vipassanā.

If you take a Christian position, the monastic orders, beginning with the Benedictines, had to look after themselves. So they separated out of society, and this meant there was a cleavage. The monks were way over there up in the hills having a great time, and we were down here. But we didn't have to support them, and they didn't have to teach us. They were fine.
The belief was, of course, that they were doing a lot of prayer — especially these enclosed orders. They were praying for the world, and there was a belief that this was doing good.

Then you get this amazing saint, Saint Francis. When I saw his patchwork robe made out of sackcloth, that was quite a little moment for me. Here was a man — and the people who followed him, including Saint Clare — who went right back to absolute poverty and depended upon lay people for their well-being. Although the early friars could work; they could do labour and get money for it. And the connection between friars and lay people was immediate.

Coming back to the Buddhist order in the West, there is this bit of a problem — especially if Western people haven't really taken on the idea of building up a little piggy bank so you get to a better place when you die. Most people are very iffy about that. First of all, the whole idea of a karmic bank account is very iffy. The whole idea that you're going to go anywhere after death is very iffy. Supposedly you don't. And I've given all this stuff away. That's fair.
When the order went to China, beggars were looked down upon. You could not beg. So a famous abbot said: no work, no food. In China, the monks began to grow their own food.

There's a lovely little film called something White Clouds, where this American has gone to China and gotten to know these Buddhist monks way up in the hills, who knows where they are. And there they are, planting their own little crops and living secluded lives. Every so often they come down into the village to collect stuff. I don't know whether they receive it by generosity or not.
You can see that the order had to change there in terms of looking after itself. But in so doing, it lost its connection with lay people. There wasn't that exchange of generosity anymore.

Why would you want to support me if I didn't teach? If you knew that I was just in this little hut in the middle of Wales, messing about, doing a bit of meditation, reading — and I turn up at your doorstep with a bowl — what would you say? What's this about?

And that often happened. It happened to the Buddha. Some Brahmin really berated him and said, 'You're useless. You don't do anything. You come here begging for food. Who the hell are you?' And the Buddha said, 'Well, thank you for the present. I offer it back to you.' So he went on his way. It wasn't as though everywhere Buddhist monks went, they were thought to be worthy of donation.

If you look upon a monastery as a spiritual hospital — well, your body can be diseased, and we're very happy to help people overcome physical diseases. We have no problem with that. There are mental illnesses, and we're very happy to help people who are mentally disturbed and need help.

But there are also spiritual diseases. By which I mean those people who are longing for liberation from suffering — who actually feel their suffering strongly enough that life as we generally know it has become meaningless.

One of the signs of somebody moving towards a spiritual life is realising the meaninglessness of life. What's the point of living? Here we are, we've been born. We haven't a clue where we're going. We fill our lives with doing. We give ourselves a sense of purpose by doing something for others, because others say how wonderful we are. And looking back, as the old comedian's saying goes, life's hard and then you die.

That sense of meaninglessness moves towards despair. It moves towards the existentialist philosophers like Albert Camus, who felt that we've got life, evening can be merry, because tomorrow you die. That's the end of that.

People in the West who turn up with a spiritual disease have usually come across this emptiness — emptiness meaning the meaninglessness of their lives. And somehow there's something in them which says, I don't believe it. I don't believe that life as it exists is purposeless. There is something, there is some purpose in it. And the purpose can't be out there, because at some point I'm going to have to turn into myself and die. So the purpose must be within me somewhere. That's your spiritual search.

That's the Buddha. The Buddha is living in an age where the obsession was rebirth — constant rebirth. How to get out of this constant rebirth? If you did something wrong you might be reborn as a dog, and then the next life you're born as a human being, and the next life you're born into a happy realm, then you fall from that and come back as a human being. One of the terrors of the age was this idea that you'd be constantly reborn. How could you get out of that in any permanent way?

There were a few people who were materialists. They believed that when you die, that's the end of it — that belief was going on too. But there was also this search for something beyond this constant saṃsāra, this constant onward going.

So when the Buddha left on his search, he would have been in that state of meaninglessness, in a state of existential crisis. It manifests in the tale, the legend of him seeing somebody sick, somebody old, somebody dying, a corpse, and somebody sitting under a tree — a samaṇa, a mendicant. And all the time it's put in this mythological way: he asks, what is this? He's never seen this sort of thing before. Does it happen to me? Yes. Sickness, ageing and death. And then the possibility of some sort of answer.

That's what a monastery is really about. Or any collection of people who gather together for spiritual purpose — to find what reason there is for living, for being alive. And worse, what's the reason for dying? Is there a purpose in our death?

I hope you continue to put some money in the box so that eventually I can escape to the Brecon Beacons in my little hut, knowing full well that there's always going to be a plate of spaghetti for me when I get down the hill.

There's a lovely little saying here. In the discourse which records the last moments of his life before he dies, the Buddha says to Ānanda that he feels happy because he has established the four congregations: the monks, the nuns, the lay women and the lay men. He felt that they were now rooted well enough that they would continue with the teachings after he died.
And at this point Ānanda says to him — this is Ānanda talking to the Buddha — that he feels half the spiritual life is good companionship, admirable friendship, admirable camaraderie.

And the Buddha says, oh no, Ānanda. It's the whole of the spiritual life. The whole of the spiritual life is this interdependency. It's the whole idea that you're not on your own and that you do need other people. It's not just the teacher. You need other people to support you in your practice.

That's why we come together here. We form a saṅgha and we help each other. There's no need to speak, no need to talk — something is happening within us simply by being together. And it's so much easier, shall I say, when we know that everybody else in the room is also suffering. It's much easier to sit here with all this pain and horror, knowing that somebody right next to me is probably going through worse.
Mind you, we normally think they're having a great time. You come in and everybody's sitting like this, perfectly still, and you think, oh my goodness.
That's the accent the Buddha puts on friendship when it comes to the spiritual life.

Consider now: if you were a Muslim living somewhere like Saudi Arabia, and you wanted to follow those five daily prayers. Right there, in the middle of your coffee break, you got your mat out and off you went. And everybody thought, oh, that's a good idea — and they all joined you.
Can you imagine doing that in London? Or in Dublin? Imagine getting your mat out in the middle of a workplace, in the middle of a factory, and just saying, 'I'm going to do my prayers now.'
You can see that living in a society which has lost that general grounding for the spiritual life makes it just that much more difficult for us.

Just when we want to maintain mindfulness, when we want not to go out and get drunk and party, we're considered unsociable. Part of our behaviour becomes unsociable because we're not joining in the regular behaviour of people who don't really have any spiritual vein in their lives. They don't quite know where they're going with that. All their practice is within their work — and it might be good work, too. It's not as though good work is undermined because you don't believe in a spiritual life. Far from it.
But because you don't have that immediate companionship, your practice is constantly undermined by the surroundings you're in. So it means you have to work a bit harder.

Now, going back to friendship, let me quote another passage where the Buddha says: with regard to external factors, I don't envisage any other single factor like admirable friendship as doing so much for someone in training who has not attained the heart's goal but remains intent on the unsurpassable safety from bondage. Someone who is a friend with admirable people abandons what is unskillful and develops what is skillful.
That's the point of a friend. They support you when you're being skillful and don't support you when you're being unskillful. And so you get that support.

When you read the scriptures, by the way — I've translated it as 'somebody,' but it's always 'monk.' The commentaries tell us this stands for anybody who's listening to the Dhamma. It's like the phrase 'all hands on deck.' What is it, a synecdoche?
When it comes to the lay people, there are a few discourses where the Buddha explains how we ought to live. And here he is talking directly to lay people.

What is meant by admirable friendship? The Buddha describes it this way: a lay person, in whatever town or village they may dwell, spends time with householders and householders' sons, young or old, who are advanced in virtue. They talk with them, engage them in discussions. They emulate consummate conviction in those who are consummate in conviction, consummate virtue in those who are consummate in virtue, consummate generosity in those who are consummate in generosity, and consummate discernment in those who are consummate in discernment.

This is called admirable friendship. In other words, it means seeking out people who have these virtues: generosity, faith, virtue, and discernment. To be with people who have confidence fills you with confidence. To be with people who are generous makes you want to be generous. To be with people who are virtuous, you naturally want to be virtuous like them. And people who are wise—you tend to listen to them.
Then the Buddha goes on. In another passage he describes what good friendship is.
Good friendship inspires love.
Good friendship inspires respect.
Good friendship inspires emulation.
A good friend listens.
A good friend gives good advice.
A good friend is able to enter into deep issues with you.
And a good friend helps you not waste your resources—your money, your time.
But the Buddha goes on even further. He says a friend gives what is hard to give. That's the test, isn't it? A friend gives what is hard to give.
So you know your friends immediately.
A friend does what is hard to do.
A friend has patience with harsh speech. A friend has patience with wrongdoing.
If you love somebody, you have to presume they're going to hurt you. If you love somebody and think they're never going to hurt you, you'll be very upset when they do.

If you presume that your friend, your family member, will at some point do something painful for you—at whatever level—then you can easily forgive them and re-establish the friendship. But if you think they shouldn't do that, if you think a friend or family member should not behave like that towards you, then that's it. You've cut off friendship. That's the end of it. Take them to court.

A good friend is someone you can confide in, someone who keeps your confidences. That's a very deep trust, depending on what you're sharing. Often it's helpful to talk out a problem—it can be relieving to the heart to share a burden with somebody. But you wouldn't do it if you thought they'd be off telling the very person you're complaining about.
And a good friend is free of contempt—someone who does not despise you. Someone who may envy you but does not act upon it. Someone who may feel an occasional jealousy but does not act upon it.
These are good pointers, aren't they? Don't you think so? I rather like them. They point out the difficulty of friendship.

Sometimes we get the impression that Buddhism is really all about vipassanā and meditation—that basically that's what it is. We forget there is a whole Eightfold Path. Vipassanā belongs to the last three factors: right effort, right concentration, right mindfulness. But these feed into the first one, which is right understanding.

Once you've got right understanding, it has to become systemic. It has to manifest in our attitudes. And as soon as it begins to manifest in our attitudes, it must express itself: right speech, right action, right livelihood. The whole thing flows around upon itself. One feeds upon the other. It's a spiral, turning and turning.
In this way, within a small length of time, if we practise with severe dedication, we will no doubt be liberated.
I can only hope that my words have been of some assistance—and that by your severe devotion to the practice, you may be liberated from all suffering sooner rather than later.
